content:
Hi, I want to know status of interrupt binding. (I'm trying to write my driver and I need.)
As far as know, irq is in rust/kernel/irq.rs - rust branch (frozen)
Is any work on rust-next branch?
If no-one working in this, maybe I can contribute.

To my knowledge, no one is working on that yet. But could you maybe share more information about the driver you're working on? Currently most of the wrappers need a real usage for upstream acceptance, so better make people aware the use case.

Boqun Feng 말함:

To my knowledge, no one is working on that yet. But could you maybe share more information about the driver you're working on? Currently most of the wrappers need a real usage for upstream acceptance, so better make people aware the use case.

Thanks for inform! I currently working on RTC driver (rtc-xgene).
It could be binding with devm_request_irq, but I just want more deeper with rust! :)
Are there any other subsystems you need bindings for to make that work, i2c or so?
There is some irq support in the nvme tree https://github.com/metaspace/linux/tree/nvme
It is rebased on mainline but not upstream

Trevor Gross 말함:

Are there any other subsystems you need bindings for to make that work, i2c or so?

For now, an irq subsystem is enough. And to be honest, I don't know much about linux rust yet. (So I don't know what I need)
I'll try to implement it first and ask if there's anything I need.

Andreas Hindborg 말함:

There is some irq support in the nvme tree https://github.com/metaspace/linux/tree/nvme

Thanks I'll rebase on this. Also I'll share after RTC device work.

Andreas Hindborg 말함:

There is some irq support in the nvme tree https://github.com/metaspace/linux/tree/nvme

Oh I can see C style device ioremap, writel, readl -> io_mem.rs?
Before I start implementation want some advices from you.
Can I just follow the style here?(Resources, IoMem)
Are there any other updated methods or discussions (about device register r/w)?

Kwanghoon Son said:

Andreas Hindborg 말함:

There is some irq support in the nvme tree https://github.com/metaspace/linux/tree/nvme

Oh I can see C style device ioremap, writel, readl -> io_mem.rs?
Before I start implementation want some advices from you.
Can I just follow the style here?(Resources, IoMem)
Are there any other updated methods or discussions (about device register r/w)?

The code is quite old and was not reviewed on the mailing list. I think it was @Wedson Almeida Filho who wrote it originally. I did not pay much attention to the code other than rebasing it and making sure that it works for the NVMe driver. Reviews and comments are very welcome :) If you find any issues or if you have ideas for how to do things more ergonomically, please don't hesitate to send suggestions.

I have on my todo list to reach out to other contributors in the community to check if there is some overlap between the patches that I keep for NVMe and the patches they keep for USB, net, SPI, etc. If there is overlap, I think we should establish a common branch for the patches. Maybe rust-pci, rust-dma etc., or maybe combined if that makes more sense. I would like to get the quality of the patches to a point where they can be submitted, but I have limited time.

@Wedson Almeida Filho @Gary Guo

Hi, I wrote small rtc driver. io_mem.rs was particularly helpful in implementing the rtc driver (small read time function). One question is, is there a special reason why io_mem is not listed on the rust-next?
If it is just busy for other task would you mind if I'm trying upstream?

https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commit/f9e2f7e1a4468a11aa432e755911e577cd2e1706
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commit/5444b8b2ad903d4d85bc01519b16fea3555b1cb7
A lot of things don't get upstreamed unless there is something in-tree that uses it. Is your rtc driver for a new part that you might look to upstream at some point?

Trevor Gross 말함:

A lot of things don't get upstreamed unless there is something in-tree that uses it. Is your rtc driver for a new part that you might look to upstream at some point?

Thanks for reply! Yes. this is real device rtc-xgene.c. (platform dev)
Of course, should avoid unnecessary patches being upstream. However, register reading and writing is very basic and essential. If the device (written in rust) that is actually proven tries to go up together, the patch seems to be exponentially large.(platform dev, devm, alloc all things) In fact, isn't it already proven in the rust branch?

However, register reading and writing is very basic and essential.

Yeah, and io_mem.rs is likely easy to upstream, but nevertheless we need to have an expected user in-tree.

If the device (written in rust) that is actually proven tries to go up together, the patch seems to be exponentially large.

It does not necessarily need to go all in at the same time -- please see the last bullet point of https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules

In fact, isn't it already proven in the rust branch?

That drivers (in general) can be written in Rust was proven by the rust branch, yeah. But that does not mean everything in rust will land upstream as-is. Some may need a rework, some may need documentation, some may be requested to be changed by upstream maintainers, etc.

One question that may help: is your Rust driver equivalent to the existing C one? i.e. can it be consider a reimplementation?

It is easier if it is new hardware, but if not, then we could ask maintainers of the RTC subsystem what they think about having such a "Rust reference driver". If they are OK with it, then we may submit the abstractions.

Miguel Ojeda 말함:

If the device (written in rust) that is actually proven tries to go up together, the patch seems to be exponentially large.

It does not necessarily need to go all in at the same time -- please see the last bullet point of https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules

This link is helpful. I missed thanks!
